title: Percent Applications
blurb: Percent change, discounts, markups, tax, tips, simple interest, and percent error — the percents that show up on receipts.

lessons.title: The Percent Equation
lessons.concreteComparison: Three questions walk into class: “What is $30\%$ of $150$?”, “$45$ is what percent of $150$?”, “$45$ is $30\%$ of what?” In Grade 6 they felt like three different tricks. They're ONE equation with a different blank each time: $\text{part} = \text{percent} \times \text{whole}$. Your new equation-solving skills turn every percent question into fill-in-the-blank.
lessons.objective: Use part = percent × whole as a single equation and solve it for any of its three unknowns.
concept: Every percent statement has three actors: the **part**, the **percent** (as a decimal or fraction), and the **whole**. They're bound by one equation: $\text{part} = \text{percent} \times \text{whole}$.
“What is $30\%$ of $150$?” — the part is missing: $p = 0.30 \times 150 = 45$.
“$45$ is what percent of $150$?” — the percent is missing: $45 = r \times 150$, so $r = \frac{45}{150} = 0.30 = 30\%$.
“$45$ is $30\%$ of what?” — the whole is missing: $45 = 0.30 \times w$, so $w = \frac{45}{0.30} = 150$.
Convert the percent to a decimal BEFORE it enters the equation ($30\% = 0.30$), and convert back at the end if the answer is a percent.
lessons.keyIdea: One equation, three blanks: $\text{part} = \text{percent} \times \text{whole}$ — solve for whichever is missing.

facts.title: The percent equation
facts.explanation: All three classic percent questions are this equation with a different unknown.

facts.title: Percent enters as a decimal
facts.explanation: Divide by 100 on the way in; multiply by 100 on the way out.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying by the raw percent number.
commonMistakes.correction: $30\%$ of $150$ is $0.30 \times 150$, not $30 \times 150$. Percents must convert to decimals (or fractions) before multiplying.

commonMistakes.text: Dividing by the part instead of the percent when finding the whole.
commonMistakes.correction: “$45$ is $30\%$ of what?” solves as $w = 45 \div 0.30$, not $0.30 \div 45$. The whole is always BIGGER than the part when the percent is under 100.

steps.title: Two scales, one marker
steps.teach: The top scale reads percent, the bottom reads value — one marker reads both at once. Slide it and feel how part, percent, and whole lock together.

steps.title: Three questions, one machine
beats: Missing percent → divide part by whole.
Missing whole → divide part by percent.

steps.title: Percents convert at the door
beats: Mixing forms is the #1 percent bug.

steps.title: Which blank?
steps.prompt: “$12$ is $15\%$ of what number?” What's the answer?

steps.title: Fill the blank
steps.intro: Identify part, percent, whole — then solve for the empty seat.

examples.prompt: A \$60 jacket is taxed at $8\%$. How much is the tax?
Riley answered $34$ of $40$ questions correctly. What percent?
A team won $18$ games — $60\%$ of its season. How many games were played?

steps.title: Per centum
steps.body: “Percent” is Latin *per centum* — “per hundred” — and the $\%$ symbol evolved from a sloppy abbreviation of *cento* in 15th-century Italian merchant ledgers: the letters melted into two circles and a slash. Merchants needed it because taxes, interest, and exchange fees were all quoted per hundred — the same three applications you're about to master.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Convert percents to decimals on the way in, back to percents on the way out.
Missing whole? Divide the part by the percent.

lessons.title: Percent Change & Multipliers
lessons.concreteComparison: A sneaker drops from \$80 to \$60 — that's a $25\%$ decrease. Here's the Grade 7 upgrade: instead of computing the change and subtracting, ONE multiplication does it all. A $25\%$ decrease is $\times 0.75$; a $25\%$ increase is $\times 1.25$. Multipliers turn two-step percent problems into one-step ones — and they're the key to everything that follows.
lessons.objective: Compute percent change from before/after values, and use multipliers (1 ± r) for increases and decreases.
concept: **Percent change** always measures against the ORIGINAL: $\text{percent change} = \frac{\text{change}}{\text{original}} \times 100\%$.
From \$80 to \$60: change is $20$, original is $80$, so $\frac{20}{80} = 25\%$ decrease.
The **multiplier** view: increasing by $r\%$ multiplies by $(1 + \frac{r}{100})$; decreasing by $r\%$ multiplies by $(1 - \frac{r}{100})$. A $25\%$ off sale is simply $\times 0.75$.
Multipliers reverse cleanly: if a price AFTER a $25\%$ discount is \$60, the original was $60 \div 0.75 = 80$. Dividing by the multiplier undoes the change.
Warning: a $25\%$ decrease is NOT undone by a $25\%$ increase — $80 \to 60 \to 75$. The base changed, so the percents don't cancel.
lessons.keyIdea: Percent change = change ÷ ORIGINAL; increase → ×(1 + r), decrease → ×(1 − r); divide by the multiplier to go backwards.

facts.title: Change over original
facts.explanation: The original is always the denominator — never the new value.

workedExamples.statement: A price falls from \$80 to \$60. Find the percent change.
Attendance rose from $240$ to $300$. Find the percent increase.
A \$44 game is discounted $35\%$. Use a multiplier to find the sale price.

commonMistakes.text: Dividing the change by the NEW value.
commonMistakes.correction: From \$80 to \$60, the change is measured against the original \$80: $\frac{20}{80} = 25\%$, not $\frac{20}{60} = 33\%$.
$80 \times 0.75 = 60$, but $60 \times 1.25 = 75 \neq 80$. To reverse a change, DIVIDE by its multiplier: $60 \div 0.75 = 80$.

tryIt.statement: A plant grew from $16$ cm to $22$ cm. Find the percent increase.
What single multiplier applies a $12\%$ increase, and what is $50$ after it?

steps.title: Before, after, percent
steps.teach: Two bars: before and after. The highlighted gap is the change — and the percent it represents is measured against the ORIGINAL bar. Reveal it and check your instinct.

steps.title: Original in the basement
beats: Same gap, different original — different percent.

steps.title: Meet the multiplier
beats: To go BACKWARDS, divide by the multiplier.
After a $25\%$ discount you pay \$60 → original $60 \div 0.75 = 80$.

steps.title: Pick the multiplier
steps.prompt: Which single multiplier applies an $18\%$ decrease?
steps.explanation: Decrease by $18\%$ leaves $82\%$: multiply by $0.82$. ($\times 0.18$ would leave only 18%!)

steps.title: Change, both directions
steps.intro: Find the original, pick the direction, one clean division or multiplication.

examples.prompt: A video's views jumped from $1{,}500$ to $2{,}400$. Percent increase?
After a $40\%$ discount, headphones cost \$33. What was the original price?
A \$150 rent rises $8\%$. New rent, in one multiplication?

problems.prompt: A town's population fell from $2{,}000$ to $1{,}700$. Percent change?
After a $30\%$ increase, a stock costs \$91. Original price?

steps.title: The asymmetric elevator
steps.body: Lose $50\%$ and you need $+100\%$ just to get back — a stock that halves must DOUBLE to recover. Investors call this “the asymmetry of loss,” and it's pure denominators: the way down measures against the big number, the way back against the small one. Multiplier arithmetic ($\times 0.5$ then $\times 2$) makes it obvious; percent-speak hides it.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Percent change divides by the ORIGINAL, always.
Reverse a change by DIVIDING by its multiplier — never by applying the opposite percent.

lessons.title: Markup, Discount & Tax
lessons.concreteComparison: A shop buys a hoodie for \$20 (cost), marks it up $80\%$ to \$36 (price tag), then runs a $25\%$ off sale (\$27), and the register adds $8\%$ tax (\$29.16). Four numbers, three percent moves — and each move is one multiplier. Retail is a multiplier assembly line, and you're about to read it like the manager.
lessons.objective: Apply markup, discount, and sales tax — separately and chained — using multipliers.
concept: **Markup** raises cost to selling price: an $80\%$ markup multiplies the cost by $1.80$. Stores mark up to cover rent, wages, and profit.
**Discount** lowers the tag price: $25\%$ off multiplies by $0.75$.
**Sales tax** rides on the FINAL price at the register: $8\%$ tax multiplies by $1.08$.
Chained changes MULTIPLY: cost $\times 1.80 \times 0.75 \times 1.08$ is the whole story in one line: $20 \times 1.80 \times 0.75 \times 1.08 = 29.16$.

Order between discount and tax doesn't change the total ($\times 0.75 \times 1.08 = \times 1.08 \times 0.75$) — multiplication commutes — but stores must apply tax last by law, and the intermediate numbers differ.
lessons.keyIdea: Markup, discount, and tax are multipliers — chain them by multiplying, and read any missing number by dividing.

facts.title: Three multipliers
facts.explanation: Markup up, discount down, tax up — each is one clean factor.

facts.title: Chains multiply
facts.explanation: A whole retail story compresses into one product.

workedExamples.statement: A store buys a hoodie for \$20 and marks it up $80\%$. Find the tag price.
The \$36 hoodie goes on sale at $25\%$ off, then $8\%$ tax is added. Find the register total.
A laptop rings up at \$648 after a $20\%$ discount and $8\%$ tax. What was the tag price?

commonMistakes.correction: A 25% discount then 8% tax is NOT a 17% discount. Chains multiply: $0.75 \times 1.08 = 0.81$ — an effective 19% off.

commonMistakes.text: Taking the markup percent of the SELLING price.
commonMistakes.correction: An 80% markup is 80% of the COST: $20 \times 1.80 = 36$. Percent moves always name their base — read carefully.

tryIt.statement: A gadget costs the store \$45 and is marked up $60\%$. Tag price?
A \$25 meal gets $6\%$ tax. Register total?

steps.title: The price bar, sliced
steps.teach: Watch a price split into “you pay” and “discount” — then flip the mode and watch tax extend the bar past the tag. Every retail move is a resize of this bar.

steps.title: Divide to rewind
beats: Know the total, want the tag? Divide by the tax multiplier.
Rewind the assembly line one factor at a time.

steps.title: Chain reaction
steps.prompt: A \$50 item is $30\%$ off, and $10\%$ tax applies. Register total?

steps.title: Retail, decoded
steps.intro: Name each multiplier, then chain or rewind.

examples.prompt: A bookstore buys novels at \$8 and sells at \$14. What's the markup percent?
Sneakers: tag \$90, sale $40\%$ off, tax $5\%$. Total?
What SINGLE percent change equals $20\%$ off followed by $20\%$ tax?
steps: Not zero! The two 20s use different bases.

problems.prompt: A café marks up \$2.50 cost by $120\%$. Menu price?
After $15\%$ off, a jacket costs \$68. Tag price?
Which is cheaper for a \$100 item: “$30\%$ off” or “$20\%$ off then an extra $15\%$ off”?
problems.explanation: Stacked: $0.80 \times 0.85 = 0.68$ → \$68. Single: $0.70$ → \$70. The stacked discounts win by \$2.

steps.title: The eternal sale
steps.body: Regulators in several countries have fined stores for “perpetual discounts” — tags marked up just to show a big percent off. The math tells the story: a \$40 item marked up to \$80 and sold “50% off” costs… \$40. Chained multipliers ($\times 2 \times 0.5 = \times 1$) expose the trick instantly, which is why consumer-protection agencies employ people who do exactly this arithmetic.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Markup ↑ from cost, discount ↓ from tag, tax ↑ at the register — each one multiplier.
Chains multiply; percents never simply add across different bases.
Rewind any step by dividing by its multiplier.

lessons.title: Simple Interest
lessons.concreteComparison: You lend your cousin \$200, and they agree to pay $5\%$ interest per year. After $3$ years they owe the \$200 back PLUS $200 \times 0.05 \times 3 = 30$ dollars of interest. That's **simple interest**: the same charge every year, computed on the original amount. One formula runs savings accounts, loans, and every “...% per year” sentence: $I = Prt$.
lessons.objective: Use I = Prt to compute interest, total repayment, and any missing variable.
concept: **Simple interest** charges the same amount every period: $I = Prt$, where $P$ is the **principal** (starting amount), $r$ is the yearly **rate** as a decimal, and $t$ is the **time** in years.
The **total** after $t$ years is the principal plus its interest: $A = P + I = P(1 + rt)$.
The formula solves for ANY missing letter with your equation skills: given $I$, $P$, and $t$, the rate is $r = \frac{I}{Pt}$.
Time must be in YEARS: $6$ months enters as $t = 0.5$, and $30$ months as $t = 2.5$.
lessons.keyIdea: $I = Prt$ and $A = P(1 + rt)$ — the rate enters as a decimal, the time in years.

facts.title: The formula
facts.explanation: Interest = principal × rate × time, the same slice every year.

facts.title: Total owed
facts.explanation: Principal plus all the interest, in one multiplier.

workedExamples.statement: \$200 is lent at $5\%$ simple interest for $3$ years. Find the interest and the total repayment.
A savings account holds \$1,500 at $4\%$ simple interest. How much interest after $6$ months?
\$800 grew to \$920 in $2$ years of simple interest. Find the rate.

commonMistakes.correction: $t$ is in YEARS: 6 months is $t = 0.5$, not $t = 6$. Divide months by 12 before entering the formula.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting the interest when the question asks the total.
commonMistakes.correction: $I = Prt$ is only the interest. “How much do they owe?” wants $A = P + I$.

tryIt.statement: \$600 at $3.5\%$ simple interest for $4$ years. How much interest?
How long does \$500 at $6\%$ take to earn \$90 of interest?

steps.title: The same slice every year
beats: Year 2: the SAME \$10 — interest is always on the original.
(Banks usually compound — interest on interest — that's Grade 10's exponential story.)

steps.title: One multiplier again
beats: The multiplier idea from percent change, recycled.

steps.title: Run the formula
steps.prompt: \$400 at $6\%$ simple interest for $2.5$ years. How much INTEREST?

steps.title: All four unknowns
steps.intro: I, P, r, or t — the formula serves whichever is missing.

examples.prompt: What principal earns \$45 at $5\%$ in $3$ years?
A \$2,000 loan at $9\%$ runs $8$ months. Interest?
Total repayment on \$1,200 at $7\%$ for $5$ years?

steps.title: Interest workout
steps.intro: Decimals in, years in, then multiply or solve.

problems.prompt: At what rate does \$250 earn \$40 in $4$ years?
Total owed on \$500 at $8\%$ for $3$ years?

steps.title: Interest is older than coins
steps.body: Loans with interest appear in Sumerian records from around 3000 BC — farmers borrowed seed grain and repaid with a share of the harvest, a thousand years before metal money existed. The Sumerian word for interest, *mash*, literally means “calf”: lend a herd, and the interest is the calves it bears. $I = Prt$ is the cuneiform calf-count, formalized.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Total owed: $A = P(1 + rt)$ — one multiplier.
Months convert to years; any missing letter yields to equation skills.

lessons.title: Percent Error
lessons.concreteComparison: You estimate the jar holds $250$ jellybeans; the count reveals $280$. Your guess was off by $30$ — but is that a GOOD guess? Off by 30 on 280 is different from off by 30 on 60. **Percent error** measures the miss relative to the true value: $\frac{30}{280} \approx 10.7\%$ — pretty sharp, actually.
lessons.objective: Compute percent error as |measured − actual| ÷ actual, and interpret what it says about accuracy.
concept: **Percent error** compares a measured (or guessed) value to the actual one: $\text{percent error} = \frac{|\text{measured} - \text{actual}|}{\text{actual}} \times 100\%$.
The absolute value makes error direction-blind: guessing $250$ when the truth is $280$, or $310$ when the truth is $280$, are both off by 30 — both about $10.7\%$.
The denominator is the ACTUAL value — the truth, not your guess. Just like percent change measures against the original, percent error measures against reality.
Percent error is the language of precision: a $\pm 1\%$ scale is trustworthy for cooking; a $\pm 10\%$ one is not for medicine.
lessons.keyIdea: $\text{percent error} = \frac{|\text{measured} - \text{actual}|}{\text{actual}} \times 100\%$ — the miss, sized against the truth.

facts.title: The formula
facts.explanation: Absolute miss over the true value.

facts.title: Truth in the basement
facts.explanation: The denominator is reality, not your guess.

workedExamples.statement: You estimated $250$ jellybeans; the jar held $280$. Find the percent error.
A scale reads $2.3$ kg for a package that truly weighs $2.5$ kg. Percent error?
A recipe predicts $24$ cookies; you baked $30$. Percent error of the prediction?

commonMistakes.text: Dividing by the guess instead of the actual.
commonMistakes.correction: Estimated 250, actual 280: divide the miss by 280 (truth), not 250 (guess). $\frac{30}{280}$, not $\frac{30}{250}$.
The absolute value bars are part of the formula. Guessing high or low, the ERROR is positive — direction is a separate remark.

tryIt.statement: A thermometer reads $37.8°$ when the true temperature is $36$. Percent error?
You predicted $40$ visitors; $50$ came. Percent error?

steps.title: Guess vs truth
beats: Measured (or guessed) value: what you said.

steps.title: A miss needs a scale
beats: Off by 30 on a jar of 280 → sharp guess ($10.7\%$).
Off by 30 on a jar of 60 → wild guess ($50\%$).
Raw error can't tell these apart; percent error can.
Same logic as percent change — relative beats absolute.

steps.title: Size the miss
steps.explanation: $\frac{|45-50|}{50} = \frac{5}{50} = 10\%$. (Dividing by the guess 45 gives the wrong 11.1%.)

steps.title: Error in the wild
steps.intro: Miss first, then divide by the truth.

examples.prompt: A map app estimated $28$ minutes; the drive took $35$. Percent error?
A 3D printer aims for a $12.5$ mm part but produces $12.3$ mm. Percent error?
Two students guess a rope's length (true: $8$ m). Ana says $7$ m; Bo says $9.2$ m. Who's relatively closer?

problems.prompt: Estimated \$60 for groceries; the bill was \$75. Percent error?
A package labeled $500$ g actually weighs $520$ g. Percent error of the label?
Whose guess is relatively better: off by $4$ on a true value of $25$, or off by $6$ on a true value of $60$?

steps.title: The 2% cosmic argument
steps.body: Astronomers measuring the universe's expansion rate disagree by about $8\%$ depending on method — and that “small” percent error is one of the biggest crises in modern cosmology, spawning hundreds of papers. In science, percent error isn't a grade on your homework; it's a signal that either your ruler or your theory is broken. Sometimes the theory loses.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Divide by the TRUTH, and keep the absolute value bars.
Relative error compares guesses fairly across different sizes.

lessons.title: Percents in the Wild
lessons.concreteComparison: Dinner: \$48.60 of food, $18\%$ tip, split three ways. A paycheck: \$640 base plus $4\%$ commission on \$2,500 of sales. A jacket: two coupon strategies to compare. Adult life fires percents at you in bursts of two and three — this lesson is target practice with everything from the unit at once.
lessons.objective: Solve multi-step percent problems mixing tips, commission, taxes, discounts, interest, and comparisons.
concept: **Tips** work like tax — a percent of the bill, added on: an $18\%$ tip on \$48.60 makes the total $48.60 \times 1.18$.
**Commission** is percent-of-sales pay: total pay $=$ base $+$ rate $\times$ sales.
Mental anchors make percents fast: $10\%$ is a decimal shift; $5\%$ is half of that; $1\%$ is two shifts. $18\% = 10\% + 5\% + 1\% + 1\% + 1\%$.
For comparisons — coupons, plans, deals — compute BOTH endings. The percent that sounds bigger doesn't always win; the base it applies to matters more.
lessons.keyIdea: Stack the tools: multipliers for changes, part = percent × whole for slices, mental anchors for speed.

facts.title: Tip/total in one step
facts.explanation: An 18% tip makes the total 118% of the bill.

facts.title: Commission pay
facts.explanation: Percent-of-sales income rides on top of a fixed base.

workedExamples.statement: A \$48.60 dinner gets an $18\%$ tip and is split among $3$ friends. How much does each pay?
A salesperson earns \$640 base plus $4\%$ commission on \$2,500 of sales. Total pay?
Which saves more on a \$120 jacket: coupon A ($25\%$ off) or coupon B (\$20 off then $10\%$ off the rest)?

commonMistakes.text: Tipping on the taxed total by accident of habit.
commonMistakes.correction: Convention varies, but math doesn't: decide the BASE first. An 18% tip on \$48.60 of food is $48.60 \times 0.18$ regardless of what tax did.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming the bigger percent is the better deal.
commonMistakes.correction: 25% off \$120 ties with “\$20 then 10%”. Percents ride on bases — always compute both endings before choosing.

tryIt.statement: A \$36 haircut with a $15\%$ tip — total?
Base pay \$500 plus $6\%$ commission on \$3,200 of sales. Total pay?

steps.title: Tip the bar
steps.teach: Set the tip percent and watch the bill's bar extend past the original — a tip is a percent increase you choose at the table.

steps.title: Commission anatomy
beats: More sales, more pay — the base is the safety net.

steps.title: Total the table
steps.prompt: A \$60 dinner, $20\%$ tip, split between $2$ people. Each pays…

steps.title: Bursts of two and three
steps.intro: One tool per step; keep the bases straight.

examples.prompt: A realtor earns $2.5\%$ commission on a \$180,000 house. Commission?
Lunch \$14.50, tax $8\%$, tip $20\%$ on the pre-tax food. Total?
Store A: \$80 headphones at $30\%$ off. Store B: \$75 at $25\%$ off. Which is cheaper?

steps.title: Adult-life speed run
steps.intro: Choose the tool, mind the base.

problems.prompt: Sales of \$1,800 at $5\%$ commission plus \$300 base. Total pay?
\$200 at $6\%$ simple interest for $2$ years, versus a flat \$25 fee. Which costs less?

steps.title: The no-tip nations
steps.body: In Japan and South Korea, tipping is rare — and can even read as rude — while in the U.S. a 15–20% tip is effectively part of the server's wage. Same arithmetic, opposite customs. Travelers who master the 10-5-1 anchor trick can adapt to any country's percent culture in seconds; the math is the only universal part.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Tips and tax are chosen percent increases; commission is percent-of-sales pay.
The 10-5-1 anchors make mental percents fast.
In comparisons, compute both endings — bases beat big-sounding percents.

practice.statement: A phone's price fell from \$250 to \$210. Find the percent decrease.
A \$65 dress is $20\%$ off, plus $5\%$ tax on the sale price. Register total?
After a $15\%$ markup, a bike sells for \$276. What did the store pay?
\$750 at $4\%$ simple interest for $30$ months. Find the interest.
You estimated a movie at $120$ minutes; it ran $150$. Percent error?
A waiter's Friday: \$82 base plus $18\%$ tips on \$460 of tables. Total earnings?

testYourself.statement: A stock rose $25\%$ on Monday, then fell $20\%$ on Tuesday. Net change from the start?
testYourself.solution: $1.25 \times 0.80 = 1.00$ — exactly back to the start: $0\%$ net.

testYourself.statement: After an $8\%$ tax, a receipt total is \$97.20. What was the pre-tax price?
How long does \$1,600 at $4.5\%$ simple interest take to earn \$216?
A carpenter cut a board to $84$ cm; the plan called for $80$ cm. Percent error?
Which is the better deal on a \$90 order: free shipping (worth \$7.50) or $10\%$ off?
